analogy: Think of "From Trade to Territory" through the chapter's own opening images: From Trade to Territory The Company Establishes Power 2 Aurangzeb was the last of the powerful Mughal rulers. He established control over a very large part of the territory that is now known as India. After his death in 1707, many Mughal governors (subadars) and... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 10 OUR PASTS – III, FROM TRADE TO TERRITORY 11, 12 OUR PASTS – III, FROM TRADE TO TERRITORY 13. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
From Trade to Territory is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that from Trade to Territory The Company Establishes Power 2 Aurangzeb was the last of the powerful Mughal rulers.

The text explains that he established control over a very large part of the territory that is now known as India.

The text explains that after his death in 1707, many Mughal governors (subadars) and big zamindars began asserting their authority and establishing regional kingdoms.

The text explains that as powerful regional kingdoms emerged in various parts of India, Delhi could no longer function as an effective centre.

The text explains that by the second half of the eighteenth century, however, a new power was emerging on the political horizon – the British.

The text explains that did you know that the British originally came as a small trading company and were reluctant to acquire territories?

The text explains that how then did they come to be masters of a vast empire?

The text explains that in this chapter you will see how this came about.

The text explains that 1 – Bahadur Shah Zafar and his sons being arrested by Captain Hodson After Aurangzeb there was no powerful Mughal ruler, but Mughal emperors continued to be symbolically important.

The text explains that in fact, when a massive rebellion against British rule broke out in 1857, Bahadur Shah Zafar, the Mughal emperor at the time, was seen as the natural leader.

The text explains that once the revolt was put down by the company, Bahadur Shah Zafar was forced to leave the kingdom, and his sons were shot in cold blood.

The text explains that 10 OUR PASTS – III East India Company Comes East I n 1 6 0 0 , t h e E a s t India Company acquired a charter from the ruler of England, Queen Elizabeth I, granting it the sole right to trade with the East.

The text explains that this meant that no other trading group in England could compete with the East India Company.

The text explains that with this charter, the Company could venture across the oceans, looking for new lands from which it could buy goods at a cheap price, and carry them back to Europe to sell at higher prices.

The text explains that the Company did not have to fear competition from other English trading companies.

The text explains that mercantile trading companies in those days made profit primarily by excluding competition, so that they could buy cheap and sell dear.

The text explains that two years later, it bribed Mughal officials into giving the Company zamindari rights over three villages.

The text explains that it also persuaded the Mughal emperor Aurangzeb to issue a farman granting the Company the right to trade duty free.

The text explains that aurangzeb’s farman, for instance, had granted only the Company the right to trade duty free.

The text explains that they refused to grant the Company concessions, demanded large tributes for the Company’s right to trade, denied it any right to mint coins, and stopped it from extending its fortifications.

The text explains that accusing the Company of deceit, they claimed that the Company was depriving the Bengal government of huge amounts of revenue and undermining the authority of the nawab.

The text explains that if this could be done without conquest, through the help of local rulers who were willing to grant privileges, then territories need not be taken over directly.

The text explains that the Diwani allowed the Company to use the vast revenue resources of Bengal.

The text explains that source 2 The Nawab complains In 1733 the Nawab of Bengal said this about the English traders: When they first came into the country they petitioned the then government in a humble manner for liberty to purchase a spot of ground to build a factory house upon, which was no sooner granted but they built a strong fort, surrounded it with a ditch which has communication with the river and mounted a great number of guns upon the walls.

The text explains that many died an early death in India due to disease and war, and it would not be right to regard all of them as corrupt and dishonest.

The text explains that they were often seen as upstarts and social climbers in British society and were ridiculed or made fun of in plays and cartoons.

The text explains that company Rule Expands If we analyse the process of annexation of Indian states by the East India Company from 1757 to 1857, certain key aspects emerge.

The text explains that the Company rarely launched a direct military attack on an unknown territory.

The text explains that source 3 Imagine that you are a young Company official who has been in India for a few months.

The text explains that as long as the prince acts in perfect subservience, and does what is agreeable to the residents, that is, to the British Government, things go on quietly; they are managed without the resident appearing much in the administration of affairs … but when anything of a different nature happens, the moment the prince takes a course which the British Government think wrong, then comes clashing and disturbance.

The text explains that james Mill (1832) Tipu Sultan – The “Tiger of Mysore” The Company resorted to direct military confrontation when it saw a threat to its political or economic interests.

The text explains that this process, however, did not go unchallenged.

The text explains that they fought a prolonged war with Afghanistan between 1838 and 1842, and established indirect Company rule there.

The text explains that the Doctrine of Lapse The final wave of annexations occurred under Lord Dalhousie who was the Governor-General from 1848 to 1856.

The text explains that this time the British had an added argument – they said they were “obliged by duty” to take over Awadh in order to free the people from the “misgovernment” of the Nawab!

The text explains that from 1772 a new system of justice was established.

The text explains that pollard, 1789 When Warren Hastings went back to England in 1785, Edmund Burke accused him of being personally responsible for the misgovernment of Bengal.

The text explains that soldiers were Here is a passage from Edmund Burke’s eloquent opening speech during t h e i m p e a c h m e n t o f Warren Hastings: I impeach him in the name of the people of India, whose rights he has trodden under his foot and whose country he has turned into a desert.

The text explains that source 5 “I impeach the common enemy and oppressor of all.” Dharmashastras – Sanskrit texts prescribing social rules and codes of behaviour, composed from c.

The text explains that you will read about this revolt in Chapter 5.

The text explains that the arrival of new steam technology in the early nineteenth century also aided this process.

The text explains that by 1857, the Company came to exercise direct rule over about 63 per cent of the territory and 78 per cent of the population of the Indian subcontinent.

The text explains that combined with its indirect influence on the remaining territory and population of the country, the East India Company had virtually the whole of India under its control.

The text explains that match the following: Diwani Tipu Sultan “Tiger of Mysore” right to collect land revenue faujdari adalat Sepoy Rani Channamma criminal court sipahi led an anti-British movement in Kitoor 2.

The text explains that how would you have reacted to the stories of British conquests?

The text explains that remember that you would have read about the immense fortunes that many of the officials were making.

The text explains that what attracted European trading companies to India?

The text explains that what were the areas of conflict between the Bengal nawabs and the East India Company?

The text explains that how did the assumption of Diwani benefit the East India Company?

The text explains that in what way was the administration of the Company different from that of Indian rulers?

The text explains that describe the changes that occurred in the composition of the Company’s army.

The text explains that after the British conquest of Bengal, Calcutta grew from small village to a big city.

Source: NCERT Class 8 Our Pasts III, Chapter 2: From Trade to Territory

Do not add outside examples to From Trade to Territory; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of From Trade to Territory, which statement is supported by the source text about FROM TRADE TO TERRITORY 19?
options: As trade expanded, the Company persuaded merchants and traders to come and settle near the factory.
By 1696, it began building a fort around the settlement.
Two years later, it bribed Mughal officials into giving the Company zamindari rights over three villages.
One of these was Kalikata, which later grew into the city of Calcutta or Kolkata as it is known today.

options: It also persuaded the Mughal emperor Aurangzeb to issue a farman granting the Company the right to trade duty free.
The Company tried continuously to press for more concessions and manipulate existing privileges.
Aurangzeb’s farman, for instance, had granted only the Company the right to trade duty free.
But officials of the Company, who were carrying on private trade on the side, were expected to pay duty.
